name: Walter Page
abstract: Walter Sylvester Page (February 9, 1900 – December 20, 1957) was an American jazz multi-instrumentalist and bandleader, best known for his groundbreaking work as a double bass player with Walter Page's Blue Devils and the Count Basie Orchestra.

concerts.label: Benny Goodman and His Swing Orchestra
Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Poll Winners Jazz Concert

about_quotes.speaker: John Beal
about_quotes.evidence: I saw an interview with him and he said when he was 13 years old he went to hear Basie’s band and he could hear Walter Page
about_quotes.doc_title: John Beal interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 18, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: I had played with Jo Jones and Walter Page in St. Louis with a band called Jeter-Pillars
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry Edison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Freddie Green
about_quotes.evidence: that was a rhythm section like there's never been a rhythm section like That in a band before... Jo Jones and Walter Page and Basie was doing something else

about_quotes.speaker: Helen Humes
about_quotes.evidence: The personnel is ... Walter Page ... And I'll never forget one time, we was playing ... there was the bass up in there. He done fell off the stand.

about_quotes.speaker: Harry "Sweets" Edison
about_quotes.evidence: And had it not been for Count Basie, I wouldn’t be here with you... Jo Jones. Walter Page. Freddie Green, Buck Clayton, sitting next to him
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry "Sweets" Edison interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Schaap
about_quotes.evidence: I even met Walter Page at six... I remember the one with Page, on the street... 'Hello Mr. Page.' And he shook my hand and that was it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Schaap interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 9, 2003
